required.title: [For Mentor] What Every Body Is Saying
required.author: Joe Navarro

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] My Body Sends a Signal
suggested.author: Natalia Maguire

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] The Definitive Book of Body Language
supplementary.author: Allan and Barbara Pease

competence_markers.description: The child can greet an unfamiliar adult with a handshake, eye contact, and a spoken greeting without prompting
The child maintains eye contact during conversations with adults and peers as a default behavior
The child demonstrates upright, composed posture when standing and sitting in social settings without constant reminders
The child can remain physically still during a five-minute conversation or read-aloud without fidgeting or disengaging
The child shows awareness that their face communicates emotions and can identify basic emotions on others' faces
The child demonstrates appropriate conversational distance and adjusts when the other person appears uncomfortable
The child can describe in their own words why body language matters — that the body talks even when the mouth does not
The child engages with narration prompts with personal examples and genuine reflection, not rote answers

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me what your body says when you stand tall versus when you slouch.
narration_prompts.detail: How do you feel different? How do other people see you differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe how to shake someone's hand well.
narration_prompts.detail: Walk me through every step. Why does each step matter?
What does it tell the other person when you look at them? What does it tell them when you look away?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time this week when you noticed someone's body language.
narration_prompts.detail: What were they doing? What do you think they were feeling? How could you tell?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest part of being still when someone else is talking?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it hard? Why is it worth doing anyway?

narration_prompts.prompt: If you could not use any words — only your body — how would you show someone that you are happy to see them? That you are paying attention? That you are confident?

cross_connections.description: Body awareness, coordination, and proprioception developed through physical training transfer directly to social presence. The child who knows where their body is in space moves through social settings with greater control and intention.

The stillness and composure trained in this packet are direct extensions of the self-regulation developed in IC1.1. The child who can follow routines and control impulses can also control their body in social settings.

Body language and verbal communication work together. The child who speaks clearly but slouches and avoids eye contact undermines their own words. These two sub-domains develop in parallel and reinforce each other.

The greeting, eye contact, and physical presence skills developed here are the physical platform on which conversation skills are built. A child who cannot make eye contact will struggle to sustain a conversation.

Maintaining composure under social attention — being watched, being the center of a room — is an early form of the resilience developed in IC3. The child who can be still and present under attention is building emotional resilience.
The handshake, greeting, and physical courtesy trained here overlap directly with the etiquette foundations in 3.3.5. Physical presence is the body's expression of good manners.

content.why_this_matters:
Before you say a single word, people are already deciding what they think about you. They are watching how you stand, where your eyes look, what your hands are doing, and whether your face looks friendly, scared, or bored. This happens fast — faster than you can say your own name.

This is not unfair. It is how human beings work. For thousands and thousands of years, people have been reading each other's bodies to figure out who is paying attention, who is confident, who is nervous, and who is dangerous. Your body is talking all the time, whether you know it or not.

The good news is that you can learn what your body is saying. You can learn to stand in a way that shows you are paying attention. You can learn to look people in the eye when you talk to them. You can learn to shake hands like someone who means it. You can learn to sit still when someone else is talking, because your stillness tells them you care about what they are saying.

These are not tricks. They are habits — good habits that you build now, while you are young, so that by the time you are older they are as natural as breathing. The person who learns these habits early has an advantage that lasts their entire life. That person walks into a room and people notice — not because they are loud, but because they are present.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Eye contact as a social skill** — the habit of looking at the person you are speaking to and the person speaking to you, with steady and engaged attention
2. **Posture awareness** — understanding that how you stand and sit communicates something to others, and developing the habit of upright, composed posture
3. **The handshake** — a firm, confident handshake with eye contact and a spoken greeting, practiced until automatic
4. **Facial awareness** — the beginning understanding that your face expresses emotions whether you intend it to or not, and that others read your face constantly
5. **Personal space** — awareness that different people have different comfort zones for physical proximity
6. **Stillness** — the ability to be physically still and attentive when someone else is speaking
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Body Is Always Talking

Right now, as you are listening to this, your body is saying something. If you are sitting up straight with your eyes on the person reading to you, your body is saying: "I am here. I am paying attention. I care about what you are saying." If you are slouched down, looking at the floor, fidgeting with something in your hands, your body is saying: "I am bored. I do not want to be here. I am not listening."

You might be listening perfectly well while slouching. But the person talking to you does not know that. They can only see what your body is doing. And what your body is doing is the first thing they believe — before your words, before your intentions, before anything else.

This is true for every human being on Earth. It has been true for as long as people have existed. Before humans had language — before anyone could speak a single word — they had bodies. They communicated with their eyes, their posture, their gestures, and their faces. We still do. Every single day.

### Part 2: Eyes — The Most Important Signal

When you look at someone while they are talking to you, you are telling them three things: I see you. I hear you. You matter to me.

When you look away — at the floor, at the wall, at something across the room — you are telling them: I am not interested. I would rather be somewhere else.

Eye contact does not mean staring. Staring makes people uncomfortable. Eye contact means looking at the person's face — mostly their eyes — in a natural, relaxed way. You look at them while they talk. You look at them while you talk. You can look away briefly to think, and then you come back to their eyes.

This is one of the most powerful social skills you will ever learn, and it is one of the simplest. The child who looks adults in the eye when speaking to them is remembered as confident, respectful, and trustworthy. The child who looks at the floor is remembered as shy, uncertain, or disengaged — even if they are none of those things.

**Practice:** Every time you talk to an adult this week, look at their eyes. Every time an adult talks to you, look at their eyes. Notice how it feels. Notice how they respond to you.

### Part 3: How You Stand and Sit

Stand up right now. Let your shoulders drop. Let your arms hang. Slouch a little. How does that feel?

Now pull your shoulders back. Stand up as tall as you can. Put your feet flat on the ground, about as wide apart as your shoulders. Let your arms hang naturally at your sides. How does that feel?

Most people feel different in those two positions. The slouch feels easy but small. The tall posture feels bigger, stronger, more awake. Other people see the difference too. The person who stands tall looks like they belong wherever they are. The person who slouches looks like they wish they were somewhere else.

This is not about being stiff like a soldier. It is about being upright like a person who is present and paying attention. When you sit, the same rule applies: sit up, feet on the floor, hands visible and still. Not rigid — just present.

**Practice:** When you sit for meals, sit up straight with both feet on the floor. When you stand to talk to someone, stand tall. Notice the difference in how you feel and how people respond to you.

### Part 4: The Handshake

A handshake is one of the oldest greetings in human history. When you shake someone's hand, you are saying: "I greet you as an equal. I am glad to meet you. I am someone worth knowing."

Here is how to shake hands well:

1. **Stand up** if you are sitting. You always stand to shake hands.
2. **Look the person in the eye.**
3. **Extend your right hand** with your thumb pointing up and your fingers together.
4. **Grip firmly** — not crushing, not limp. A firm grip says confidence. A limp grip says uncertainty. A crushing grip says you are trying too hard.
5. **Shake two or three times** — from the elbow, not the shoulder.
6. **Say something:** "Nice to meet you," or "Hello, I'm [your name]."
7. **Let go.**

That is it. Seven steps, and most adults cannot do all seven well. You are going to practice until you can do all seven without thinking about it.

**Practice:** Shake hands with every new adult you meet. Shake hands with family members when they arrive. Practice with your mentor until the handshake is smooth, firm, and automatic.

### Part 5: Your Face Is a Billboard

Your face is broadcasting your feelings to everyone around you, all the time. When you are happy, people can see it. When you are angry, people can see it. When you are bored, people can see it. When you are scared, people can see it.

You do not need to fake your feelings. No one is asking you to smile when you are sad or look serious when you are happy. But you do need to know that your face is visible to others. If you are sitting in a conversation looking bored — even if you are not bored — the person talking to you will think you do not care about what they are saying.

The simplest version of this: when someone is talking to you, let your face show that you are interested. A slight nod. A small smile when something is funny. A serious look when something is serious. Your face should match what is happening in the conversation.

**Practice:** With your mentor, look at pictures of people's faces in magazines or books. What emotion is each face showing? How can you tell? Then look in a mirror and make different faces: happy, serious, interested, surprised. See what your own face does when you feel different things.

### Part 6: The Space Between People

When you talk to someone, how close do you stand? This matters more than you might think.

If you stand too close, the other person feels crowded. They will back up, lean away, or feel uncomfortable — even if they do not say anything. If you stand too far away, it feels like you do not want to be in the conversation. You look disconnected.

The right distance is usually about an arm's length — close enough to talk comfortably, far enough that neither person feels crowded. But this changes depending on the situation: you stand closer to family and close friends, and farther from people you just met.

The skill is not memorizing a distance. The skill is paying attention. Watch the other person. If they lean back or step away, you are too close. If they lean in or seem to strain to hear you, you are too far. Adjust.

**Practice:** During conversations this week, notice the space between you and the other person. Are you standing at a comfortable distance? Is the other person comfortable? Pay attention to their body — it will tell you.

### Part 7: Stillness — The Hardest Skill

When someone else is talking, your job is to be still. Not frozen — still. Your hands are not touching things. Your feet are not kicking. Your body is not wiggling. Your eyes are not wandering around the room.

This is hard. It might be the hardest thing in this entire packet. Being still requires you to control your body even when your body wants to move. But stillness communicates something powerful: respect. When you are still while someone speaks, you are telling them with your whole body that what they are saying matters to you.

This does not mean you cannot move at all. You can nod. You can shift your weight. You can gesture when it is your turn to talk. But the fidgeting — the pen-clicking, the leg-bouncing, the looking-around — that stops. Because every fidget says to the speaker: "I am not really here."

**Practice:** During read-aloud time or family conversations, practice being physically still for increasing periods. Start with two minutes. Build to five. Build to ten. It gets easier. The stillness becomes comfortable. And the people around you will notice the difference.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Greeting Practice [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Every time the child meets a new person or greets someone at the start of the day, they practice the full greeting: stand up, make eye contact, shake hands (where appropriate), and say a clear hello with the person's name. The mentor creates opportunities for this — introducing the child to friends, neighbors, shopkeepers, and other adults. After each greeting, a brief check-in: "How did that feel? Did you remember eye contact? Was your handshake firm?"

### Study 2: The Silent Movie [Early in the Packet]
Watch a short video clip with the sound off. What can the child tell about the people in the video just from their bodies? Who looks confident? Who looks nervous? Who looks bored? Who looks like they are in charge? Discuss how the child figured this out. This makes the concept of body language concrete and observable.

### Study 3: Posture Check-Ins [Ongoing]
At random moments throughout the day, the mentor calls "posture check." The child notices their current posture and adjusts if needed. This is not punitive — it is awareness training. Over weeks, the child begins to catch their own slouching before being told. The self-correction is the goal.

### Study 4: The Mirror Study [Mid-Packet]
The child stands in front of a full-length mirror and practices: their handshake (with the mentor standing beside them), their greeting, standing tall, sitting attentively, and making different facial expressions. Seeing yourself as others see you is a powerful awareness tool.

### Study 5: The Space Experiment [Mid-Packet]
With family members or willing friends, the child experiments with conversational distance. Stand very close — how does it feel? Stand very far — how does it feel? Find the comfortable distance. Try with different people. Notice that different people have different comfort zones.

### Study 6: The Stillness Challenge [Ongoing]
The child practices being completely still during read-aloud time. Start with short periods and gradually extend. The mentor notes when the child is still without effort — that is the marker of developing competence. Celebrate the progress: "You sat still for the entire chapter today. That is a real skill."

### Study 7: People Watching [Late Packet]
In a public place (a park, a restaurant, a waiting room), the child observes other people's body language with the mentor. What does that person's posture say? Does that person look comfortable? Who in the room looks the most confident? Why? This builds the habit of observing nonverbal communication in real life.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Eye Contact
**Developing well:** The child looks at adults and peers during conversations as a default. They do not need reminders. Eye contact feels natural and comfortable to them.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently looks at the floor, their hands, or elsewhere during conversations despite repeated practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child anxious about eye contact, or simply untrained? Anxiety requires gentler progression (start with looking at the person's forehead or nose, then gradually move to eyes). Simple lack of training responds to consistent practice and positive reinforcement.

### 2. Posture
**Developing well:** The child stands and sits upright in social settings without being told. They self-correct when they notice slouching. They look comfortable standing tall, not rigid.

**Developing slowly:** The child slouches habitually and does not self-correct. Posture reminders produce brief compliance followed by immediate return to slouching.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is there a physical issue (core strength, fatigue, seating that does not fit the child's size)? Address the physical before assuming the behavioral. If the child is physically capable, increase the frequency of posture check-ins without making them feel criticized.

### 3. The Handshake
**Developing well:** The child shakes hands firmly, with eye contact and a verbal greeting, and does so without being prompted. It looks natural, not performative.

**Developing slowly:** The handshake is limp, eye contact drops during the handshake, or the child forgets the verbal greeting. The child needs prompting to shake hands.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child practiced enough? Twenty repetitions is not enough. A hundred is closer. Practice with many different adults — variety builds adaptability.

### 4. Stillness
**Developing well:** The child can sit through a five-to-ten-minute conversation or read-aloud without fidgeting. They appear attentive and engaged. Stillness looks comfortable, not forced.

**Developing slowly:** The child fidgets constantly — touching things, bouncing legs, looking around, picking at clothing. Even short periods of stillness are difficult.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child getting enough physical activity? A child who has been sitting all day has a much harder time being still than one who has run, played, and moved. Schedule body language practice after physical activity when possible. Also consider whether the material is age-appropriate — a child who cannot sit still during a boring conversation may sit perfectly still during a fascinating story.

### 5. Facial Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can identify emotions on others' faces and is developing awareness that their own face communicates. They show appropriate facial responses during conversations (interest, amusement, seriousness).

**Developing slowly:** The child shows a flat or incongruent facial expression during conversations. They cannot identify basic emotions on others' faces.

**Diagnostic questions:** Some children, particularly those on the autism spectrum, process facial expressions differently. If facial awareness seems significantly delayed, consult with a developmental professional. For neurotypical children who are simply unaware, the mirror study and people-watching exercises in the student packet build awareness gradually.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making It Feel Like Punishment
If "posture check" or "look at their eyes" becomes a constant nag delivered with frustration, the child associates good body language with being corrected — and they will resist it. Keep corrections brief, specific, and warm. Praise the behavior you want more than you correct the behavior you do not want.

### Expecting Perfection
A six-year-old who shakes hands well and makes eye contact most of the time is well ahead of most adults. Do not hold out for every marker every time. Look for trending improvement, not flawless performance.

### Practicing Only at Home
Body language skills practiced only with family in familiar settings are not real skills yet. The child needs to greet unfamiliar adults, maintain composure in new environments, and practice stillness in settings that are not perfectly comfortable. Create these opportunities intentionally.

### Ignoring Your Own Body Language
If the child sees you looking at your phone during conversations, slouching during meals, or avoiding eye contact with service workers, they will learn that body language rules apply to children but not adults. Model the behavior you teach.

### Overcomplicating for the Age
Stretching Branches is about basic habits: eye contact, posture, handshake, stillness, and facial awareness. Do not introduce concepts like mirroring, microexpressions, or proxemics theory. The child needs practice, not theory. Theory comes in Weathering Storms.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Standing Tall vs. Slouching
**Strong:** The child describes the physical difference in how they feel and connects it to how others perceive them. They may use their own words: "When I stand tall I feel like I'm ready for something. When I slouch I feel like I'm hiding."
**Weak:** "I dunno, it's just different." No connection between physical posture and its social meaning.

### Prompt 2: The Handshake
**Strong:** The child can walk through the steps in order and explain why each matters. They may demonstrate while narrating.
**Weak:** "You just grab their hand and shake it." No detail, no awareness of the components.

### Prompt 3: Eye Contact
**Strong:** The child explains that eye contact tells the other person you are paying attention and that you respect them. They may offer a personal example.
**Weak:** "Because you're supposed to." No understanding of why eye contact matters.

### Prompt 4: Noticing Body Language
**Strong:** A specific observation from their week with detail: "The man at the store was crossing his arms and looking away. I think he was bored or didn't want to talk."
**Weak:** Cannot recall any observation. Has not been practicing awareness.

### Prompt 5: Stillness
**Strong:** Honest about the difficulty and able to articulate why it matters despite being hard. "It's hard because my body wants to move. But when I'm still, people can tell I'm listening."
**Weak:** "It's easy" (unlikely to be true) or "I don't know why it matters."
